topic: random history question — Battle of Memphis

turns.content: hey random question. what do you know about the Battle of Memphis

The Battle of Memphis was a Civil War naval battle on June 6, 1862. Union forces attacked a Confederate fleet on the Mississippi River right in front of the city. It was actually pretty short — lasted about an hour and a half. The Union destroyed or captured most of the Confederate ships, and Memphis surrendered that same morning. Thousands of civilians watched it happen from the bluffs. What made you curious about it?
drove past one of those historical markers today on the way to a job. said something about a naval battle and I thought that cant be right. Memphis aint on the ocean

Ha, yeah it's easy to forget how important the Mississippi River was militarily. During the Civil War, controlling the river was a huge strategic priority. Memphis was a key Confederate port, so taking it gave the Union control of a big stretch of the river. The whole battle happened right on the water in front of downtown.
so people just stood there and watched a battle happen

Yeah, it's wild. Reportedly thousands of Memphis residents lined up along the bluffs overlooking the river at dawn to watch the whole thing unfold. It was like spectators at an event. The Union ram boats smashed into the Confederate ships and it was over fast. Only one Union sailor was killed in the whole battle. The Confederates lost over a hundred men and almost their entire fleet.
one guy killed on the Union side. thats it?

That's the number that gets cited — one Union fatality, a man named Charles Ellet Jr. actually survived the battle but died of a wound from it a couple weeks later. He was the commander of the ram fleet. It was incredibly lopsided. The Confederate fleet was outgunned and outmaneuvered from the start.
man I been living here my whole life and never knew any of that. drove past that marker a hundred times probably

That's how it goes with local history — you pass by it every day without a second thought. There's actually a decent amount of Civil War history around Memphis if you ever get curious. Fort Pillow is north of the city and had a much more brutal battle. The city itself was under Union occupation for most of the war after that surrender.
Fort Pillow. thats the one where they killed the surrendering soldiers right

Yeah, that's the one. The Fort Pillow massacre in 1864. Confederate forces under Nathan Bedford Forrest attacked the fort and killed a large number of Union soldiers — many of them Black troops — who were trying to surrender. It's one of the most controversial events of the entire war. Very different from the Memphis battle.
yeah I remember hearing about that one in school. heavy stuff. anyway thanks for the history lesson man. learned something today
Anytime. Next time you drive past that marker you'll know the full story. Take it easy.
